main_summary.summary: Joint interview with Joseph (Joe) Howell and Embry Howell, conducted December 13, 2015 by David Cline of the Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project. Joe Howell was born 1942 in Nashville, Tennessee, son of a Nashville City Banks president, raised in the Belle Meade neighborhood; he contracted polio in 1952 and spent six months at Warm Springs, an experience he credits with developing empathy. Embry Howell was born 1945 in Bethesda, Maryland and raised in Davidson, North Carolina, where her father D. Grier Martin served as treasurer and later president of Davidson College and worked behind the scenes to integrate the institution. As a Davidson College senior in spring 1964, Joe attended the Third Great American Revolution conference at Bryn Mawr and Haverford, then organized the March on Charlotte, a signature-collection drive supporting the Civil Rights Act of 1964, conducted with Barber-Scotia and Johnson C. Smith students. The Howells later attended Union Theological Seminary, where Charles Sherrod recruited them for SNCC work in Southwest Georgia in summer 1966. They arrived in Albany during the John Lewis–Stokely Carmichael split over the role of white organizers. Allard Lowenstein became a close associate; Dennis Sweeney, Lowenstein's later assassin, was a Union classmate's roommate.
key_themes: Southern white students entering the civil rights movement
Charles Sherrod and SNCC Southwest Georgia Project
Black Power transition and white-organizer tensions
main_summary.historical_significance:
The Howells' joint testimony documents the recruitment pipeline that brought white Southern students from Davidson College and Union Theological Seminary into SNCC's Southwest Georgia project during the pivotal 1966 transition to Black Power. Their account of arriving in Albany to be greeted with 'go home, whitey, we don't need you' captures the John Lewis–Stokely Carmichael split as it was experienced by white workers on the ground, while Embry's observation that black SNCC organizers nonetheless treated her with personal civility complicates the binary public narrative.

Joe's March on Charlotte in spring 1964, a signature-collection campaign in support of the Civil Rights Act conducted jointly with students from Barber-Scotia and Johnson C. Smith, preserves a less-documented Southern white college mobilization moment between the March on Washington and Freedom Summer. The interview also documents the Allard Lowenstein recruitment network and the Dennis Sweeney connection through Union Theological Seminary, linking the oral history to a later tragic chapter of movement aftermath.

chapters.title: Introductions and Project Framing
chapters.summary: David Cline of the Southern Oral History Program and Virginia Tech introduces the December 13, 2015 session for the Civil Rights History Project, acknowledges videographer John Bishop and Guha Shankar of the Library of Congress, and asks Joe Howell and Embry Howell to introduce themselves before opening the conversation about their childhoods.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project oral history series

chapters.title: Embry's Davidson Childhood
chapters.summary: Embry Howell, born 1945 in Bethesda, Maryland, describes growing up in Davidson, North Carolina where her father D. Grier Martin served as treasurer and then president of Davidson College. She recounts a mother who had attended communist meetings in 1930s New York, crossing the railroad tracks to play with mill-worker children, and her father's role in admitting the first African students to Davidson around 1963.

chapters.title: Joe's Nashville Childhood and Polio
chapters.summary: Joe Howell, born 1942 in Nashville, Tennessee, describes the Belle Meade elite social world, his banker father, attendance at Montgomery Bell Academy, and contracting polio in 1952 with six months of recovery at Warm Springs. He credits the polio experience with giving him empathy for suffering people and traces an early childhood friendship with a Black playmate named Frederick that his mother eventually halted.

chapters.title: Davidson Years and Bryn Mawr Conference
chapters.summary: Joe describes Davidson College student culture, his roles as YMCA president and ODK leadership-fraternity president, and the Third Great American Revolution conference at Bryn Mawr and Haverford in spring 1964 where CORE, SNCC, and SCLC lieutenants presented to predominantly white Ivy League students. He recounts hearing Freedom Riders describe being attacked with cattle prods.

chapters.title: March on Charlotte 1964
chapters.summary: Joe describes organizing the March on Charlotte in spring 1964, a signature drive supporting the Civil Rights Act of 1964, conducted jointly with Barber-Scotia, Johnson C. Smith, and other area schools. Davidson president D. Grier Martin invited him home for a quiet conversation suggesting he abandon the march while indicating tacit support. The Nashville Banner ran the headline 'banker's son leads rights March,' and Joe became a pariah back in Nashville.

chapters.title: Allard Lowenstein Connection
chapters.summary: Joe describes Allard Lowenstein recruiting him via notes scribbled on graded tests sent special-delivery to his Davidson dorm. He sketches Lowenstein's biography, National Student Association president, Yale Law School, Eleanor Roosevelt protégé, dump-Johnson organizer, later assassinated by former student Dennis Sweeney. Sweeney was the roommate of a Union Theological Seminary friend who became a civil-liberties lawyer.

chapters.title: Union Theological Seminary and Marriage
chapters.summary: Joe describes attending Union Theological Seminary on a Rockefeller Fellowship, drawn by the legacy of Paul Tillich and Reinhold Niebuhr, then growing disillusioned and taking a year out with William (Bill) Webber's Metropolitan Urban Service Training (MUST). Embry recounts their courtship during her Bryn Mawr years and her transfer to Barnard after their marriage so they could live at Hastings Hall on the Union Theological Seminary campus.

chapters.title: Charles Sherrod Recruits for Southwest Georgia
chapters.summary: Joe and Embry describe being recruited by SNCC co-founder Charles Sherrod, who circulated through Union Theological Seminary cohorts identifying like-minded students for his Southwest Georgia project. They acknowledge they had little idea what awaited them and that people were still being killed; they accepted because the moment felt morally clear.

chapters.title: Wake Forest Training and Arrival in Albany
chapters.summary: Joe describes a pre-deployment training conference at the old Wake Forest campus run by an unidentified radical Christian ecumenical institute, which he found cult-like. The Howells then drove all night to the SNCC office in the Harlem neighborhood of Albany, Georgia, where Black SNCC organizers in white t-shirts greeted them with 'we are Black Panthers, you are roosters, Panthers eat roosters, go home, whitey, we don't need you.'

chapters.title: Black Power Split and Daily Life in Albany
chapters.summary: Joe describes the 1966 SNCC split between John Lewis (integration, retain white workers) and Stokely Carmichael (Black self-determination, white workers confuse the message at this point) playing out during their Southwest Georgia summer, with Sherrod caught in the middle. Embry observes that despite the public rhetoric, Black SNCC organizers were personally courteous to her. She describes being unable to see a Black doctor for an infection, having to go to a white physician instead. Recording truncates mid-sentence as Joe begins describing housing arrangements for the married couple.

loc_video.caption: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell oral history interview conducted by David Cline in Washington, District of Columbia, 2015 December 13
